title: Shepherds, Humility, and Standing Firm
subtitle: Elders shepherding, younger submitting, all clothed with humility against the devil

heb.paragraph: I exhort the elders among you - presbuteroi here denotes church leaders, not merely older men. Peter appeals as a fellow elder.
Shepherd the flock of God - the pastoral metaphor dominates. Elders are under-shepherds; Christ is the chief Shepherd.
Not domineering over those in your charge - leadership is service, not tyranny. Elders are examples, not overlords.

refs.note: Feed my lambs. Tend my sheep. Feed my sheep.
Pay careful attention to yourselves and to all the flock.
Woe to the shepherds of Israel who have been feeding themselves!
God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

notes.note: Peter exhorts elders as a fellow elder and witness of Christ's sufferings. Shepherd the flock of God, exercising oversight willingly, eagerly.
Not domineering but being examples. When the chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory.
Younger ones, submit to elders. All of you, clothe yourselves with humility. God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

Calvin emphasizes that elders are under-shepherds, not owners. The flock belongs to God; leaders are accountable to the chief Shepherd. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The three contrasts define good leadership: willing not compelled, eager not greedy, exemplary not domineering.
The unfading crown (amarantinos stephanos) contrasts with the fading wreaths given to athletes. This reward endures.
Peter quotes Proverbs 3:34 LXX. The opposition of God to the proud is active; he resists them.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that Peter's self-designation as fellow elder is remarkable. The apostle identifies with local church leaders. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes observes that the shepherd image connects to 2:25 where Christ is the Shepherd. Human shepherds follow his pattern. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

hist.historical: Peter writes to Christians in five Roman provinces of Asia Minor (modern Turkey) — Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia. These believers face social ostracism and verbal abuse (4:4, 14) rather than official state persecution. As 'elect exiles' (1:1), they experience the marginalization of those who no longer participate in pagan civic religion, trade guild feasts, and family worship. The letter's household codes (2:13–3:7) address how Christians should live within Roman social structures. Peter writes from 'Babylon' (5:13) — almost certainly a symbolic name for Rome. Silvanus (Silas) serves as secretary (5:12), and Mark is present (5:13). The letter likely dates to the early 60s AD, before Nero's persecution.
hist.context: Peter, fellow elder and witness of Christ's sufferings, exhorts elders to shepherd God's flock. Oversight must be willing, not forced; eager, not greedy; exemplary, not domineering. When the chief Shepherd appears, they will receive the unfading crown of glory. Younger ones are to submit to elders. All are to clothe themselves with humility toward one another, for God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble.

heb.paragraph: Humble yourselves under the mighty hand of God - active self-humbling is commanded. God will exalt at the proper time.
Be sober-minded, be watchful - spiritual alertness is necessary because an adversary prowls.
Resist him, firm in your faith - the devil is resisted by faith, not by special techniques. Firmness defeats his attacks.

refs.note: Resist the devil, and he will flee from you.
Put on the whole armor of God, that you may stand against the schemes of the devil.
Cast your burden on the LORD, and he will sustain you.
The sufferings of this present time are not worth comparing with the glory to be revealed.

notes.note: Humble yourselves under God's mighty hand so that he may exalt you at the proper time. Cast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you.
Be sober, be watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour. Resist him, firm in faith.
After you have suffered a little while, the God of all grace will restore, confirm, strengthen, establish you. To him be dominion forever.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The participle casting is dependent on humble yourselves. Casting anxiety is an expression of humility - trusting God rather than self.
The roaring lion seeks to devour - the verb is violent. Satan's intent is destruction, not mere annoyance.
The four verbs - restore, confirm, strengthen, establish - are future indicatives functioning as promises. God will act.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner notes that suffering is not meaningless. It is brief (a little while) and purposeful (God will restore). The doxology affirms God's sovereignty. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes observes that the worldwide brotherhood (v. 9) reminds isolated Christians they are not alone. Suffering is shared. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

hist.context: The exhortation continues: humble yourselves so that God may exalt you at the right time. Cast all anxiety on him because he cares. Be sober; the devil prowls like a roaring lion seeking prey. Resist him, firm in faith, knowing that the same sufferings are experienced by the worldwide brotherhood. After suffering a little while, the God of all grace will restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish. To him be dominion forever.

heb.paragraph: I have testified that this is the true grace of God - Peter summarizes the letter's message: this is genuine grace; stand firm in it.
She who is at Babylon - almost certainly a symbolic reference to Rome, the capital of empire as Babylon was in its day.
Greet one another with a kiss of love. Peace to all who are in Christ.

refs.note: Judas called Barsabbas, and Silas, leading men among the brothers.
Get Mark and bring him with you, for he is very useful to me.
Babylon the great, mother of prostitutes.
Greet one another with a holy kiss.

notes.note: By Silvanus I have written to you briefly, exhorting and declaring that this is the true grace of God. Stand firm in it. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

She who is at Babylon sends greetings, as does Mark my son. Greet one another with the kiss of love. Peace to all in Christ. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Silvanus (Silas) is described as a faithful brother. He may have served as secretary or courier or both. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

Babylon is almost universally understood as a cryptic reference to Rome. Early Christians avoided naming Rome directly in correspondence. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

schreiner.source: Thomas Schreiner, NAC 1-2 Peter/Jude — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Schreiner sees the letter summary in v. 12: this is the true grace of God. Everything in the letter - suffering, hope, holiness - is grace. Schreiner's exegesis emphasizes the eschatological dimension of the passage. The author writes with the end in view — present suffering and ethical obligation are framed by the certainty of Christ's return and the final vindication of God's people. This already-not-yet tension shapes every imperative in the text.

jobes.source: Karen Jobes, BECNT 1 Peter — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Jobes notes that the mention of Mark connects Peter to early Gospel traditions. Peter and Mark are associated in early church testimony. Jobes attends to the socio-rhetorical context of the passage, noting how the author addresses a community under social pressure. The language of identity, belonging, and hope is calibrated to sustain faith amid marginalization — the letter functions as pastoral care for believers navigating a hostile cultural environment.

hist.context: Peter concludes with personal notes. Silvanus (Silas) is the letter carrier and likely amanuensis. The letter's purpose is summarized: exhortation and testimony that this is the true grace of God - stand firm in it. Greetings come from the chosen sister in Babylon (Rome) and from Mark, described as Peter's son. The kiss of love and the blessing of peace close the letter.

themes.note: Faith is central: resist the devil firm in faith. Sovereignty appears in God's mighty hand and the promise of restoration.

lit.note: Chapter 5 addresses church leadership, spiritual warfare, and concludes with personal greetings from Babylon (Rome).

discourse.thesis: Shepherd the flock of God — humble yourselves, resist the devil, and the God of all grace will restore you.

nodes.text: Peter exhorts elders as a fellow elder and witness of Christ's sufferings: shepherd God's flock among you, exercising oversight willingly, not for shameful gain, not domineering — being examples. When the chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory.

Likewise, you who are younger, be subject to elders. Clothe yourselves, all of you, with humility — God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble. Humble yourselves under God's mighty hand; cast all your anxieties on him.

Be sober-minded, watchful. Your adversary the devil prowls like a roaring lion. Resist him, firm in faith — the same sufferings are experienced by your brotherhood worldwide. The God of all grace will himself restore, confirm, strengthen, and establish you.

By Silvanus, Peter writes briefly, exhorting and declaring this is the true grace of God — stand firm in it. The church in Babylon sends greetings, and so does Mark. Greet one another with a kiss of love. Peace to all who are in Christ.

hebtext.note: v.2's verb for elder-pastoral ministry. The shepherd-imagery draws on Jesus's John 10 good-shepherd discourse and ultimately on OT shepherd-imagery (Ezek 34, Ps 23). Peter himself received the 'feed my sheep' commission (John 21:15-17). The vocabulary connects elder-ministry to Christ's own shepherding.

v.5's compound noun (tapeinos + phrēn — low + mind). The humility-imperative governs both younger-elder relations and ecclesial-community relations broadly. The verse's quotation of Prov 3:34 ('God opposes the proud, gives grace to the humble') grounds the imperative in OT-wisdom tradition.

v.8's term for the devil. The legal-courtroom term (antidikos = opposing-counsel in legal proceedings) frames spiritual conflict as judicial-prosecution. The devil functions as accuser, not random-evil-force. Christian-resistance is properly framed as legal-defense rather than military-engagement.

ppl.name: Christian elders (presbyteroi)
ppl.text: Peter writes to the elders as fellow-elder (sympresbyteros, v.1) — emphasising shared-office rather than apostolic-superiority. The instruction is concrete: shepherd willingly not by compulsion, eagerly not for shameful gain, examples not domineering. The pastoral-ethics distill the chapter's elder-leadership principles.

ppl.name: Younger members of the community
ppl.text: v.5 addresses 'younger ones' (neōteroi) — likely younger-aged-adults rather than children — calling for submission to elders. The pastoral-instruction generalises to all-community-relations: 'all of you, clothe yourselves with humility.' The chapter's leadership-and-followership instruction is integrated, not divorced.

ppl.name: The devil as roaring lion
ppl.text: v.8's vivid 'devil prowls around like a roaring lion seeking someone to devour' is among NT's most-graphic Satanological-passages. The lion-imagery may echo Ps 22:13 + Ezek 22:25. The pastoral instruction is sober-watchfulness, with comfort that other-Christian-brothers globally share the same suffering experiences.

ppl.name: Silvanus (Silas), Mark, and 'she who is in Babylon'
ppl.text: Silvanus likely served as letter-bearer/secretary (Acts 15:22-40 records his Pauline collaboration). Mark is identified as Peter's 'son' — the Gospel-writer of Mark, whose Gospel tradition treats as Petrine-tradition-recorded. 'She who is in Babylon' likely refers to the church in Rome (using Babylon as code for the imperial capital — cf. Rev 17-18's similar usage).

rec.text: Origen's discussion of Petrine-pastoral authority frequently engages 1 Peter 5's elder-instruction. The chapter's emphasis on humble-elder-leadership has shaped Christian theology of pastoral-office across traditions, balancing genuine-authority with non-domineering character.

Augustine's classic 'Sermon on Pastors' draws extensively on 1 Peter 5's pastoral instruction (alongside Ezekiel 34 and John 10). The chapter became foundational for Western pastoral theology, particularly the warnings against shepherds who feed themselves rather than the flock.

Calvin's exposition emphasises the chapter's connection between humility and grace ('God opposes the proud, gives grace to the humble'). The Reformer treats this as pastorally-foundational principle — pride is the consistent obstacle to receiving God's grace; humility is the consistent disposition that opens reception. The principle anchors Calvin's broader sanctification-theology.

rec.who: Eugene Peterson, A Long Obedience in the Same Direction (1980)
rec.text: Peterson's contemporary pastoral-theology drew extensively on 1 Peter 5's elder-pastoral framework. The chapter shaped Peterson's vision of pastoral-ministry as long-term-faithful-service rather than charismatic-celebrity-leadership. His widely-influential reading retrieved the chapter for late-20th-century evangelical pastoral self-understanding.

thread.text: 'God opposes the proud, gives grace to the humble' is quoted verbatim by both Peter and James from Prov 3:34. The shared OT-citation across two NT writers establishes the proverb as foundational early-Christian-ethical text. The verse is among the most-cited OT-quotations in the NT epistles.

'Casting all your anxieties on him' echoes Ps 55:22's casting-cares language and parallels Jesus's Sermon-on-the-Mount don't-be-anxious teaching. The shared-anxiety-theology across OT and NT establishes that Christian-anxiety-management is genuine theological discipline grounded in trusting-divine-providence.

Peter's 'resist the devil, firm in faith' parallels Pauline armor-of-God + James's resist-the-devil instruction. Three NT writers share the resist-the-devil framework. The shared theology: resistance-not-flight is the proper Christian response to demonic-attack, but resistance is grounded in faith's stability, not autonomous-strength.

'She who is in Babylon, chosen together with you' uses Babylon as cryptic-code for Rome. Revelation's apocalyptic-Babylon (Rev 14:8; 17-18) develops the same code more elaborately. The shared-cryptic-naming reflects early-Christian self-understanding within hostile-imperial context.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBe shepherds of God’s flock that is under your care, watching over them—not because you must, but because you are willing, as God wants you to be; not pursuing dishonest gain, but eager to serve;KJVFeed the flock of God which is among you, taking the oversight thereof, not by constraint, but willingly; not for filthy lucre, but of a ready mind;NIVIn the same way, you who are younger, submit yourselves to your elders. All of you, clothe yourselves with humility toward one another, because, “God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble.”KJVLikewise, ye younger, submit yourselves unto the elder. Yea, all of you be subject one to another, and be clothed with humility: for God resisteth the proud, and giveth grace to the humble.NIVHumble yourselves, therefore, under God’s mighty hand, that he may lift you up in due time.KJVHumble yourselves therefore under the mighty hand of God, that he may exalt you in due time:NIVCast all your anxiety on him because he cares for you.KJVCasting all your care upon him; for he careth for you.NIVBe alert and of sober mind. Your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion looking for someone to devour.KJVBe sober, be vigilant; because your adversary the devil, as a roaring lion, walketh about, seeking whom he may devour:NIVAnd the God of all grace, who called you to his eternal glory in Christ, after you have suffered a little while, will himself restore you and make you strong, firm and steadfast.KJVBut the God of all grace, who hath called us unto his eternal glory by Christ Jesus, after that ye have suffered a while, make you perfect, stablish, strengthen, settle you.

coaching.tip: 'Shepherd the flock of God that is among you, exercising oversight, not under compulsion but willingly' (v. 2). Peter — himself commissioned to 'feed my sheep' (John 21:15-17) — commissions elders. The motivation matters: willingly, eagerly, as examples. Leadership style flows from inner disposition. Reluctant shepherds produce wandering sheep.

'Your adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour' (v. 8). After addressing suffering, Peter names the adversary. Resistance is communal: 'Resist him, firm in your faith, knowing that the same kinds of suffering are being experienced by your brotherhood throughout the world' (v. 9). You're not alone in the fight.

prayer_prompt: As Peter warns 'your enemy the devil prowls around like a roaring lion,' consider asking God where you are most spiritually exposed right now — and whether you have been standing firm or wandering alone.
